lesson: L14 — Volume of Rectangular Solids & Cylinders
banner: Week 16 · Prove It — Volume of Rectangular Solids & Cylinders
essential_question: Why does stacking identical layers of the base, one on top of the other, always give you a solid's exact volume?
objective: Justify why volume = area of the base × height by reasoning about stacking congruent layers, review the week's Math-U-See practice (Lesson Practice 14A & 14B), and pass the mastery checkpoint on rectangular-solid and cylinder volume and on a solid's faces, edges, and vertices.

content.prompt: Without looking, sketch a rectangular solid and a cylinder, and mark a believable length, width, and height (or radius and height) on each. Then say out loud, in your own words, why volume needs THREE measurements while area only ever needed two.

content.heading: Go over Lesson Practice 14A & 14B together
content.note: Bring your completed MUS Lesson Practice 14A/14B. We check the tricky ones: telling a cylinder's radius apart from its diameter, remembering the height runs perpendicular to the base whether that base is a rectangle or a circle, the face/edge/vertex matching questions, and the fill-in-the-blank about cubic units. Fix any you missed and note WHY.

content.heading: Defend three claims
bullets: Claim 1: volume = area of the base × height, whether the base is a rectangle or a circle. Argue it: picture stacking identical paper copies of the base, one directly on top of the next, until the stack is exactly as tall as the solid's height — how many copies of the base's area have you stacked, and why does that total exactly equal the volume?

Claim 2: a rectangular solid's volume is length × width × height — three numbers multiplied — never length × width + height or any other combination. Argue it from the base-times-height idea: what is 'the base' of a rectangular solid, and what is its area, before you ever multiply by the height?

Claim 3: a cylinder's volume is πr² × height — never πr × height or 2πr × height. Argue it: what shape is a cylinder's base, what is the actual area formula for that shape, and why would using the circle's circumference (2πr) instead of its area give you an entirely wrong kind of quantity?

content.preview: Next week, Lesson 15 — Volume of Pyramids, Cones, Prisms & Spheres — where this same base-times-height idea extends to solids that come to a point.

success_criteria.text: I can state and use the volume formulas for a rectangular solid and a cylinder without help
I can justify why volume = area of the base × height by reasoning about stacking congruent layers
I can identify a solid's faces, edges, and vertices, and measure a real container's dimensions correctly

compass.note: Curriculum/Geometry/The Salas Compass/2026-W16 Volume of Solids.md

questions.prompt: A rectangular solid has length 6 m, width 4 m, and height 3 m. Its volume is…
The volume of a geometric solid is always given in ___ units.
questions.explain: Volume fills three dimensions, so it's measured in cubic units (in³, ft³, cm³, m³) — never linear or square units.

questions.prompt: A cylinder has a radius of 6 in and a height of 9 in. Find its volume in terms of π, then estimate it as a decimal (π ≈ 3.14).
Measure the length, width, and height (or radius and height) of a real container near you right now (a mug, a shoebox, a drinking glass, a cereal box) and compute its volume.
questions.answer: Any honest measured dimensions with units and a correctly computed volume, e.g. a mug r = 3.5 cm, h = 9 cm → V = π(3.5)²(9) ≈ 346.4 cm³.
questions.explain: Rubric: dimensions measured and recorded with units, matching the solid's actual shape (1 pt) + volume correctly computed with the matching formula (1 pt).

questions.prompt: The point where edges of a rectangular solid meet is called a…
questions.explain: A vertex is a corner point where edges meet; a face is a flat surface, and an edge is the line where two faces meet.

questions.prompt: In your own words, explain why stacking identical copies of a solid's base — one on top of the next, up to the solid's full height — always gives exactly its volume, and use that idea to explain why a taller cylinder (same radius) always has more volume than a shorter one.
questions.answer: Each stacked layer has the same area as the base; stacking layers up to a height h fills the solid with no gaps and no overlap, so the total volume is exactly base-area × height. Since a taller cylinder is simply more layers of the same base area stacked higher, its volume must be greater than a shorter cylinder sharing that radius.
questions.explain: Full credit connects 'stacking congruent layers of the base' directly to why multiplying base area by height gives the exact volume, and applies it to compare two cylinders sharing a radius but differing in height.

questions.prompt: Close: rate yourself on Lesson 14 (Emerging / Proficient / Mastered) and name the one idea you'd want to revisit.
